([/kəˈtɑːniə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English") also [/-ˈteɪn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/-ˈtæn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Sicilian and Italian: [[kaˈtaːnja]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Catania.ogg "File:It-Catania.ogg")) is the second-largest municipality in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), after [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), both by area and by population.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Despite being the second city of the island, Catania is the centre of the most densely populated Sicilian conurbation,[[8]](#cite_note-8) which is among the largest in Italy. It has important road and rail transport infrastructures, and hosts [the main airport of Sicily](/wiki/Catania_Airport "Catania Airport") (fifth-largest in Italy). The city is located on Sicily's east coast, facing the [Ionian Sea](/wiki/Ionian_Sea "Ionian Sea") at the base of the active volcano [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"). It is the capital of the 58-municipality province known as the [Metropolitan City of Catania](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Catania "Metropolitan City of Catania"), which is the seventh-largest metropolitan area in Italy. The population of the city proper is 297,517, while the population of the metropolitan city is 1,068,563.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

**Catania** ([/kəˈtɑːniə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) [UK](/wiki/British_English "British English") also [/-ˈteɪn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English") also [/-ˈtæn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Sicilian and Italian: [[kaˈtaːnja]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Catania.ogg "File:It-Catania.ogg")) is the second-largest municipality in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), after [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo"), both by area and by population.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Despite being the second city of the island, Catania is the centre of the most densely populated Sicilian conurbation,[[8]](#cite_note-8) which is among the largest in Italy. It has important road and rail transport infrastructures, and hosts [the main airport of Sicily](/wiki/Catania_Airport "Catania Airport") (fifth-largest in Italy). The city is located on Sicily's east coast, facing the [Ionian Sea](/wiki/Ionian_Sea "Ionian Sea") at the base of the active volcano [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"). It is the capital of the 58-municipality province known as the [Metropolitan City of Catania](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Catania "Metropolitan City of Catania"), which is the seventh-largest metropolitan area in Italy. The population of the city proper is 297,517, while the population of the metropolitan city is 1,068,563.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

Catania was founded in the 8th century BC by Chalcidian Greeks in [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia").[[9]](#cite_note-travelplan.it-9) The city has weathered multiple geologic catastrophes: it was almost completely destroyed by a catastrophic earthquake in [1169](/wiki/1169_Sicily_earthquake "1169 Sicily earthquake").[[9]](#cite_note-travelplan.it-9) A major eruption and lava flow from nearby [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") nearly swamped the city in [1669](/wiki/1669_eruption_of_Mount_Etna "1669 eruption of Mount Etna") and it suffered severe devastation from the [1693 Sicily earthquake](/wiki/1693_Sicily_earthquake "1693 Sicily earthquake").[[9]](#cite_note-travelplan.it-9)

During the 14th century, and into the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") period, Catania was one of Italy's most important cultural, artistic and political centres.[[9]](#cite_note-travelplan.it-9) It was the site of Sicily's first [university](/wiki/University_of_Catania "University of Catania"), founded in 1434.[[9]](#cite_note-travelplan.it-9) It has been the native or adopted home of some of Italy's most famous artists and writers, including the composers [Vincenzo Bellini](/wiki/Vincenzo_Bellini "Vincenzo Bellini") and [Giovanni Pacini](/wiki/Giovanni_Pacini "Giovanni Pacini"), and the writers [Giovanni Verga](/wiki/Giovanni_Verga "Giovanni Verga"), [Luigi Capuana](/wiki/Luigi_Capuana "Luigi Capuana"), [Federico De Roberto](/wiki/Federico_De_Roberto "Federico De Roberto") and [Nino Martoglio](/wiki/Nino_Martoglio "Nino Martoglio").

Catania today is the industrial, logistical, and commercial centre of Sicily. Its airport, the [Catania–Fontanarossa Airport](/wiki/Catania%E2%80%93Fontanarossa_Airport "Catania–Fontanarossa Airport"), is the largest in Southern Italy. The central "old town" of Catania features exuberant late-[baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture"), constructed following the 1693 earthquake, and is a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The ancient [indigenous population](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples "Indigenous peoples") of Sicily, the [Sicels](/wiki/Sicels "Sicels"), named their villages after geographical attributes of their location. The [Siculian](/wiki/Siculian "Siculian") word *katane* means "grater, flaying knife, skinning place" or a "crude tool apt to pare". Other translations of the name are "harsh lands", "uneven ground", "sharp stones", or "rugged or rough soil". The latter etymologies are easily justifiable since, for many centuries following an eruption, the city has always been rebuilt within its black-lava landscape.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Around 263 BC, the city was variously known as *Catĭna* (Latin: [[ˈkatɪna]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin")) and *Catăna* (Latin: [[ˈkatana]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin"); [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Κατάνη [[katánɛː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek "Help:IPA/Greek")).[[a]](#cite_note-11) The former has been primarily used for its supposed assonance with *catina*, the Latin feminization of the name *catinus*.[[11]](#cite_note-12) *Catinus* has two meanings: "a gulf, a basin or a bay" and "a bowl, a vessel or a trough", thanks to the city's distinctive topography.

Around 900, when Catania was part of the [emirate of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily"), it was known in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") as *Balad al-fīl* (بلد الفيل) and *Madīnat al-fīl* (مدينة الفيل), respectively meaning "the Village (or Country) of the Elephant" and "the City of the Elephant".[[12]](#cite_note-13) The Elephant likely referred to the ancient lava sculpture, now placed over the fountain in Piazza Duomo. The sculpture is most likely a prehistoric sculpture that was reforged during the Byzantine Era, prized as a protective talisman against enemies, both human, natural or geologic. Another Arab toponym was *Qaṭāniyyah* (قطانية), allegedly from the Arabic word for the "[leguminous plants](/wiki/Legume "Legume")".[[13]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-14) Pulses like [lentils](/wiki/Lentil "Lentil"), beans, peas, [broad beans](/wiki/Vicia_faba "Vicia faba"), and [lupins](/wiki/Lupin "Lupin") were chiefly cultivated in the plains around the city well before the arrival of [Aghlabids](/wiki/Aghlabid "Aghlabid"). Afterwards, many Arabic agronomists developed these crops and the [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus") orchards in the area around the city. The toponym *Wādī Mūsá* (وادي موسى), or "the Valley of Moses" (from the Arabic name of the [Simeto](/wiki/Simeto "Simeto") River), was rarely used.[[13]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Catania](/wiki/Timeline_of_Catania "Timeline of Catania").

### Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Foundation")]

Around 729 BC, the ancient village of *Katane* was occupied by [Chalcidian](/wiki/Chalcis "Chalcis") Greek settlers from nearby [Naxos](/wiki/Naxos_(Sicily) "Naxos (Sicily)") along the coast. It became the Chalcidian colony of *Katánē* under a leader named Euarchos (Euarchus) and the native population was rapidly [Hellenised](/wiki/Hellenization "Hellenization").[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

[Thucydides](/wiki/Thucydides "Thucydides") states that it came into existence slightly later than Leontini (modern [Lentini](/wiki/Lentini "Lentini")), which he claims was five years after [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Italy "Syracuse, Italy"), or 730 BC.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

The settlement's acropolis was on the hill of Monte Vergine, a defensible hill immediately west of the current city centre. The port of Catania appears to have been much frequented in ancient time and was the chief place of export for the corn of the rich neighbouring plains.

### Greek Catania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Greek Catania")]

Catania was associated with the ancient legend of Amphinomos and Anapias, who, on occasion of a great eruption of Etna, abandoned all their property and carried off their aged parents on their shoulders. The stream of lava itself was said to have parted, and flowed aside so as not to harm them. Statues were erected to their honour, and the place of their burial was known as the *Campus Piorum*; the Catanaeans even introduced the figures of the youths on their coins, and the legend became a favorite subject of allusion and declamation among the [Latin poets](/wiki/List_of_Latin_language_poets "List of Latin language poets"), of whom the younger [Lucilius](/wiki/Lucilius "Lucilius") and [Claudian](/wiki/Claudian "Claudian") have dwelt upon it at considerable length.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)

The Greek polis of Catania appears to have been a local center of learning. The philosopher and legislator [Charondas](/wiki/Charondas "Charondas") (late 6th c. BC), born in Catania, putatively wrote program of laws used here and in other Chalcidic cities, both in Sicily and through [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia").[[30]](#cite_note-31) suggesting a link between Catania and other cities during this time. The poets [Ibycus](/wiki/Ibycus "Ibycus") and [Stesichorus](/wiki/Stesichorus "Stesichorus") (c. 630–555 BC) lived in Catania. The latter putatively was buried in a magnificent sepulchre outside one of the gates, therefore named *Porta Stesichoreia*. [Xenophanes](/wiki/Xenophanes "Xenophanes") (c. 570–475 BC), one of the founders of the [Eleatic](/wiki/Velia_(town) "Velia (town)") school of philosophy, also spent the latter years of his life in the city.[[31]](#cite_note-32) The first introduction of dancing to accompany the flute was also ascribed to [Andron](/w/index.php?title=Andron_of_Catania&action=edit&redlink=1 "Andron of Catania (page does not exist)"), a citizen of Catania.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

Catania appears to have remained independent until the conquest by the despot [Hieron of Syracuse](/wiki/Hiero_I_of_Syracuse "Hiero I of Syracuse"); in 476 BC, he expelled all the original inhabitants of Catania and replaced them with his subjects from the town of Leontini – said to have numbered no less than 10,000, consisting partly of [Syracusans](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") and [Peloponnesians](/wiki/Peloponnese "Peloponnese"). Hieron changed the city's name to **Αἴτνη** (*Aítnē*, Aetna or Ætna, after the nearby [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"), and proclaimed himself the [Oekist](/wiki/Oekist "Oekist") or founder of the new city. For this he was celebrated by [Pindar](/wiki/Pindar "Pindar"), and after his death he received heroic honours from the citizens of his new colony.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

A few years after the death of Hieron and the expulsion of [Thrasybulus of Syracuse](/wiki/Thrasybulus_of_Syracuse "Thrasybulus of Syracuse"), the Syracusans combined with [Ducetius](/wiki/Ducetius "Ducetius"), king of the [Sicels](/wiki/Sicels "Sicels"), to expel the newly settled inhabitants of Catania, who went on to settle in the fortress of [Inessa](/wiki/Aetna_(city) "Aetna (city)") (to which they gave the name Aetna). The old Chalcidic citizens returned to the city in 461 BC.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

The period that followed appears to have been one of great prosperity for Catania, as well as for the Sicilian cities in general.

In 415 BC, Catania became involved with the [expedition to Sicily](/wiki/Sicilian_Expedition "Sicilian Expedition") pursued by the [Athenians](/wiki/Classical_Athens "Classical Athens") to punish Syracuse. Initially the Catanaeans refused to allow the Athenians into their city, but after the latter had forced an entrance, the Athenian leader [Alcibiades](/wiki/Alcibiades "Alcibiades") made a famous speech in front of the assembly. Catania became an ally, and the headquarters of the Athenian army for the first year of the expedition, and a base of their subsequent operations against Syracuse.[[35]](#cite_note-36) After the defeat of the Athenians, Catania was again threatened by Syracuse. In 403 BC, [Dionysius I of Syracuse](/wiki/Dionysius_I_of_Syracuse "Dionysius I of Syracuse") plundered the city, sold its citizens as slaves, and repopulated the town with [Campanian](/wiki/Campania "Campania") mercenaries. However, the [Carthaginians](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") would take possession of Catania under [Himilco](/wiki/Himilco_(general) "Himilco (general)") and [Mago](/wiki/Mago_(fleet_commander) "Mago (fleet commander)"), after the nearby great naval [Battle of Catana (397 BC)](/wiki/Battle_of_Catana_(397_BC) "Battle of Catana (397 BC)") where they defeated [Leptines of Syracuse](/wiki/Leptines_of_Syracuse "Leptines of Syracuse"), and in 396 BC forcing the local Campanian mercenaries to relocate to [Aetna](/wiki/Aetna_(city) "Aetna (city)").[[36]](#cite_note-37)

[Calippus](/wiki/Calippus_of_Syracuse "Calippus of Syracuse"), the assassin of [Dion of Syracuse](/wiki/Dion_of_Syracuse "Dion of Syracuse"), held Catania for a time (Plut. *Dion*. 58); and when [Timoleon](/wiki/Timoleon "Timoleon") landed in Sicily in 344 BC Catania was subject to the despot [Mamercus](/wiki/Mamercus_of_Catane "Mamercus of Catane") who at first joined the [Corinthian](/wiki/Ancient_Corinth "Ancient Corinth") leader, but afterwards abandoned this allegiance for that of the Carthaginians. As a consequence he was attacked and expelled by Timoleon in 338 BC.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

Catania was now restored to a fragile independence; changing sides during the wars starting in 311 BC of [Agathocles of Syracuse](/wiki/Agathocles_of_Syracuse "Agathocles of Syracuse") with the Carthaginians. When [Pyrrhus](/wiki/Pyrrhus_of_Epirus "Pyrrhus of Epirus") landed in Sicily in 278 BC, Catania was the first to open its gates to him, and welcomed him with great splendor.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

### Roman rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Roman rule")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Acquedotto_romano_Valcorrente2.jpg/250px-Acquedotto_romano_Valcorrente2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Acquedotto_romano_Valcorrente2.jpg)

Roman Aqueduct at Valcorrente

During the [First Punic War](/wiki/First_Punic_War "First Punic War"), Catania was one of the first cities of Sicily to submit to the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") after their first successes in 263 BC when it was taken by [Valerius Messalla](/wiki/Manius_Valerius_Maximus_Corvinus_Messalla "Manius Valerius Maximus Corvinus Messalla").[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41) A sundial was part of the booty which was placed in the [Comitium](/wiki/Comitium "Comitium") in Rome.[[41]](#cite_note-42) Since then the city became a *civitas decumana* i.e. was subject to the payment of a tenth of its agricultural income as a tax to Rome. The conqueror of Syracuse, [Marcus Claudius Marcellus](/wiki/Marcus_Claudius_Marcellus "Marcus Claudius Marcellus"), built a gymnasium here.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

It appears to have continued afterwards to maintain its friendly relations with Rome and though it did not enjoy the advantages of a confederate city (*foederata civitas*), like its neighbours Tauromenium (modern [Taormina](/wiki/Taormina "Taormina")) and Messana (modern [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina")), it rose to a position of great prosperity under the Roman rule.

Around 135 BC during the [First Servile War](/wiki/First_Servile_War "First Servile War"), it was conquered by rebel slaves.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

One of the most serious [eruptions](/wiki/Volcanic_eruption "Volcanic eruption") of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") happened in 121 BC, when a great part of Catania was overwhelmed by streams of lava, and the hot ashes fell in such quantities in the city itself, as to break in the roofs of the houses. Catana was in consequence exempted, for 10 years, from its usual contributions to the Roman state.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The greater part of the broad tract of plain to the southwest of Catana (now called the *[Piana di Catania](/wiki/Piana_di_Catania "Piana di Catania")*, a district of great fertility), appears to have belonged, in [ancient times](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history"), to Leontini or Centuripa (modern [Centuripe](/wiki/Centuripe "Centuripe")), but that portion of it between Catana itself and the mouth of the Symaethus was annexed to Catana and must have furnished abundant supplies of grain.

[Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") repeatedly mentions it as, in his time, a wealthy and flourishing city; it retained its ancient municipal institutions, its [chief magistrate](/wiki/Chief_magistrate "Chief magistrate") bearing the title of *Proagorus*; and appears to have been one of the principal ports of Sicily for the export of corn.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In the [Sicilian revolt](/wiki/War_between_Sextus_Pompey_and_the_Second_Triumvirate "War between Sextus Pompey and the Second Triumvirate") from 44 BC [Sextus Pompeius](/wiki/Sextus_Pompeius "Sextus Pompeius") selected Sicily as his base and Catania gave in to Sextus' revolt and joined his forces. Sextus amassed a formidable army and a large fleet of warships at his base at [Messana](/wiki/Messana "Messana"), with many slaves joining from the villas of patricians. After the victory of Augustus in 36 BC much of the vast farmland in Sicily was either ruined or left empty, and much of this land was taken and distributed to members of the legions which had fought there. Catania suffered severely from the ravages but was afterwards one of the cities raised to the status of [colony](/wiki/Colonia_(Roman) "Colonia (Roman)") by Augustus which restored its prosperity through the settlement of veterans, so that in [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo")'s time it was one of the few cities in the island that was flourishing.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

Another revolt led by the gladiator Selurus in 35 BC created mayhem for a while.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

The Roman aqueduct of Catania was the longest in Roman Sicily at 24 kilometres (15 mi), starting from the springs of Santa Maria di Licodia.

It retained its colonial rank, as well as its prosperity, throughout the period of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"); so that in the 4th century [Ausonius](/wiki/Ausonius "Ausonius") in his *[Ordo Nobilium Urbium](/wiki/Ordo_urbium_nobilium "Ordo urbium nobilium")*, notices Catania and Syracuse alone among the cities of Sicily.[[48]](#cite_note-49)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/OldCatania.png/250px-OldCatania.png)](/wiki/File:OldCatania.png)

Picture of Catania in 1575

Catania was sacked by the [Vandals](/wiki/Vandals "Vandals") of [Gaiseric](/wiki/Gaiseric "Gaiseric") in 440–441. After a period under the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths"), it was reconquered in 535 by the [Eastern Roman Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_Empire "Eastern Roman Empire"), under which (aside from a short period in 550–555) it remained until the 9th century. It was the seat of the Byzantine governor of the island.

Catania was under an [Islamic emirate](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily") for two centuries, after which it fell to the [Normans](/wiki/Italo-Normans "Italo-Normans") of [Roger I of Sicily](/wiki/Roger_I_of_Sicily "Roger I of Sicily"). Subsequently, the city was ruled by a bishop-count (1072). In 1194–1197 the city was sacked by German soldiers during after the conquest of the island by emperor [Henry VI](/wiki/Henry_VI,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry VI, Holy Roman Emperor"). In 1232 it rebelled to the former's son, [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor"), who later built a massive castle, *[Castello Ursino](/wiki/Castello_Ursino "Castello Ursino")* and also made Catania a royal city, ending the dominance of the bishops.

Catania was one of the main centers of the [Sicilian Vespers](/wiki/Sicilian_Vespers "Sicilian Vespers") revolt (1282) against the [House of Anjou](/wiki/Capetian_House_of_Anjou "Capetian House of Anjou") and was the seat of the coronation of the new Aragonese king of Sicily, [Peter I](/wiki/Peter_I_of_Sicily "Peter I of Sicily"). The city remained a key Sicilian port during the [War of the Sicilian Vespers](/wiki/War_of_the_Sicilian_Vespers "War of the Sicilian Vespers"). After a civil revolt in 1299, the city was captured by an Angevin army, which [occupied the city](/wiki/Angevin_invasion_of_Sicily "Angevin invasion of Sicily") until the Angevins evacuated their holdings on Sicily in 1302.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

In the 14th century it gained importance as it was chosen by the Aragonese as a Parliament and Royal seat. Here, in 1347, it was signed the treaty of peace that ended the long [War of the Vesper](/wiki/War_of_the_Vesper "War of the Vesper") between Aragonese and Angevines. Catania lost its capital role when, in the early 15th century, Sicily was turned into a member of the [Crown of Aragon](/wiki/Crown_of_Aragon "Crown of Aragon"), and kept its autonomy and original privileges specially during the period from 1282 to 1410.

In 1434 King [Alfonso V](/wiki/Alfonso_V_of_Aragon "Alfonso V of Aragon") founded here [the *Siciliae Studium Generale*](/wiki/University_of_Catania "University of Catania"), the oldest university in the island.

### Early modern times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early modern times")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Etna_eruzione_1669_platania.jpg/250px-Etna_eruzione_1669_platania.jpg)](/wiki/File:Etna_eruzione_1669_platania.jpg)

Mount Etna erupting in 1669

With the unification of Castile and Aragon (early 16th century),[[50]](#cite_note-51) Sicily became part of the Spanish Empire. It rebelled against the foreign government in 1516 and 1647.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

In 1669 the city's surroundings suffered great material damage from the [1669 Etna eruption](/wiki/1669_Etna_eruption "1669 Etna eruption"). The city itself was largely saved by its walls that diverted most of the lava into the port. Afterwards, in 1693 the city was nearly completely destroyed by a heavy [1693 Sicily earthquake](/wiki/1693_Sicily_earthquake "1693 Sicily earthquake") and its aftershocks. The city was then rebuilt in the [Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") that nowadays characterizes it.

### Unified Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Unified Italy")]

Catania was one of the vanguards of the movement for Sicilian autonomy in the early 19th century.

In 1860 [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi")'s [expedition of the Thousand](/wiki/Expedition_of_the_Thousand "Expedition of the Thousand") conquered [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") for Piedmont from the [Kingdom of the Two Sicilies](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Kingdom of the Two Sicilies"). Since the following year Catania was part of the newly [unified Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy"), whose history it shares since then.

The first half of the twentieth century was a cycle of repeated destruction and rebuilding for the city of Catania.

During the years 1923 and 1928, Catania endured two major eruptions of Mt. Etna. The 1923 eruption lasted twenty-nine days, from 6 June until 29 June. A large lava flow occurred in the 1928 event and was the first to destroy a population center in over two hundred years.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

At the onset of World War 1, Italy was part of a defensive alliance with Germany and Austria-Hungary referred to as the Triple Alliance. After one year, Italy joined the Allied forces. Many promises made to secure Italy's help during the war were not kept resulting in stability issues throughout the country leading to the adoption of fascist ideations.[[53]](#cite_note-54) As the second World War began, the new regime opted to support Adolf Hitler, resulting in Catania and all the surrounding areas on Sicily being destroyed by Allied bombing.

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Catania was heavily bombed by the Allied air forces, owing to the presence of two of the main Axis airfields in Sicily ([Gerbini](/wiki/Gerbini_Airfield "Gerbini Airfield") and [Fontanarossa](/wiki/Catania-Fontanarossa_Airport "Catania-Fontanarossa Airport")) and for its strategically important port and [marshalling yard](/wiki/Marshalling_yard "Marshalling yard"). Altogether, the city suffered eighty-seven air raids. The heaviest took place in the spring and summer of 1943, before and during the [Allied invasion of Sicily](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Sicily "Allied invasion of Sicily"); they caused heavy damage to the city (among others, twenty-eight churches and most historic palaces suffered damage), killed 750 inhabitants and prompted most of the population to flee to the countryside.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58) After heavy fighting across eastern Sicily, Catania was eventually captured by the [British 8th Army](/wiki/British_8th_Army "British 8th Army") on 5 August 1943.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

After the conflict, and the constitution of the [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italy "Italy") (1946), Catania attempted to catch up with the economic and social development of Italy's richer northern regions. The problems faced in Catania were emblematic of those faced by other towns in the [Mezzogiorno](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"), namely a heavy gap in industrial development and infrastructures, and the threat of [the mafia](/wiki/Sicilian_Mafia "Sicilian Mafia"). This notwithstanding, during the 1960s (and partly during the 1990s) Catania enjoyed development and a period of economic, social, and cultural success. In the first decade of the 21st century, Catania's economic and social development somewhat faltered and the city is again facing economic and social stagnation. This was aggravated by the economic crisis left by the [Forza Italia](/wiki/Forza_Italia_(1994) "Forza Italia (1994)") administration of mayor Scapagnini in 2008.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

As observed by [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), the location of Catania at the foot of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") has been both a curse and a blessing. On the one hand, violent outbursts of the volcano throughout history have destroyed large parts of the city, while on the other hand the volcanic ashes yield fertile soil, especially suited for the growth of vines. (Strab. vi. p. 269)

Two [subterranean rivers](/wiki/Subterranean_river "Subterranean river") run under the city; the Amenano, which surfaces at one single point south of Piazza Duomo, and the Longane (or Lognina).[[60]](#cite_note-61)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Catania experiences a [hot-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Hot-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csa*).[[61]](#cite_note-62) The city has hot summers, one of the hottest in the whole country of Italy. Temperatures of 40 °C (104 °F) are surpassed almost every year a couple of times.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Winters are mild, with significant nighttime cooling. Precipitation is concentrated from October to March, leaving late spring and summer virtually dry. The city receives around 500 millimetres (20 inches) of rain per year, although the amount can vary greatly from year to year, from over 1,200 mm (47 in) to under 250 mm (9.8 in).

During winter nights, lows can occasionally reach below freezing. Highs under 10 °C (50 °F) may happen during winter.[[62]](#cite_note-63) Snow, due to the presence of [Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna") that protects the city from the northern winds, is an uncommon occurrence, but occasional snow flurries have been seen over the recent years, especially in the hilly districts. More recently, light snowfalls occurred on 9 February 2015, 6 January 2017 and 5 January 2019, but the last heavy snowfall dates back to 17 December 1988.

As of 2025, there are 297,517 people residing in Catania, of whom 48.3% were male and 51.7% were female. Minors total 16.6% of the population compared to pensioners who number 23.5%. This compares with the Italian average of 14.9% minors and 24.7 percent pensioners.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

The average age of Catania residents is 41 compared to the Italian average of 42.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the 10 years between 2011 and 2021, the population of Catania increased by 2.5%, while [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") as a whole declined by 0.7%.[[67]](#cite_note-timeseries-68)

The current [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") of Catania is 10.07 births per 1,000 inhabitants compared to the Italian average of 9.45 births. As of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit), 98.03% of the population was [Italian](/wiki/Italian_people "Italian people"). The largest immigrant groups come from [Sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"): 0.69%, [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"): 0.46%, and from other [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") countries (particularly from [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") and [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland")): 0.33%. There is currently a small community of Samaritans from Israel.

### City proper

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: City proper")]

The city of Catania proper (*comune di Catania*) is divided in six [administrative areas](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") called *circoscrizioni*. The current administrative setup was established in 2013, modifying previous setups dating back to 1971, 1978 and 1995.

The six areas are:

1. Centro storico
2. Picanello-Ognina/Barriera-Canalicchio
3. Borgo-Sanzio
4. San Giovanni Galermo-Trappeto-Cibali
5. Monte Po-Nesima/San Leone-Rapisardi
6. San Giorgio-Librino/San Giuseppe La Rena-Zia Lisa-Villaggio Sant'Agata

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Catane_Fontana_Dell_elefante.jpg/250px-Catane_Fontana_Dell_elefante.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catane_Fontana_Dell_elefante.jpg)

*u Liotru*, symbol of Catania

The symbol of the city is *u Liotru*, or the *[Fontana dell'Elefante](/wiki/Elephant_Fountain "Elephant Fountain")*, assembled in 1736 by [Giovanni Battista Vaccarini](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Vaccarini "Giovanni Battista Vaccarini"). It portrays an ancient lavic stone elephant and is topped by an Egyptian obelisk from [Syene](/wiki/Aswan "Aswan"). Legend has it that Vaccarini's original elephant was neuter, which the men of Catania took as an insult to their virility. To appease them, Vaccarini appropriately appended elephantine testicles to the original statue.

The [Sicilian](/wiki/Sicilian_language "Sicilian language") name *u Liotru* is a phonetic change of Heliodorus, a nobleman who, after trying without success to become bishop of the city, became a sorcerer and was therefore condemned to the stake. Legend has it that [Heliodorus](/wiki/Heliodorus_of_Catania "Heliodorus of Catania") himself was the sculptor of the lava elephant and that he used to magically ride it in his fantastic travels from Catania to [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople").[[70]](#cite_note-71) Another legend has it that Heliodorus was able to transform himself into an elephant.

The presence of an elephant in the history of Catania is surely connected to both zooarcheology and popular creeds. In fact, the prehistoric fauna of Sicily from the Upper [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic"), included [dwarf elephants](/wiki/Dwarf_elephant "Dwarf elephant"). Paleontologist [Othenio Abel](/wiki/Othenio_Abel "Othenio Abel") suggested that the presence of [dwarf elephants](/wiki/Dwarf_elephant "Dwarf elephant") in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") may be the origin of the legend of the [Cyclops](/wiki/Cyclopes "Cyclopes"). [Ancient Greeks](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), after finding the skulls of [dwarf elephants](/wiki/Dwarf_elephant "Dwarf elephant"), about twice the size of a [human skull](/wiki/Human_skull "Human skull"), with a large central [nasal cavity](/wiki/Nasal_cavity "Nasal cavity") (mistaken for a large single eye socket) supposed that they were skulls of giants with a single eye.

The *Catanian Museum of [Mineralogy](/wiki/Mineralogy "Mineralogy"), [Paleontology](/wiki/Paleontology "Paleontology") and [Vulcanology](/wiki/Vulcanology "Vulcanology")* holds the integral unburied skeleton of an *[Elephas falconeri](/wiki/Elephas_falconeri "Elephas falconeri")* in an excellent state of conservation. The first inhabitants of Etna molded such lavic artifacts to idolize the mythical proboscidian.

### Classical buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Classical buildings")]

Over the course of recorded history, the city has been buried by lava seventeen times. Beneath the present-day Catania lie the remains of the [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") city that once stood here, and below that, the earlier [Greek settlement](/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation"). Many ancient [Roman monuments](/wiki/Roman_architecture "Roman architecture") were destroyed by these repeated eruptions, but today, visitors can explore surviving ruins in the city centre, which are part of the Parco Archeologico Greco-Romano di Catania.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Catania_Parco_Villa_Bellini.jpg/250px-Catania_Parco_Villa_Bellini.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catania_Parco_Villa_Bellini.jpg)

*Villa Bellini*, one of the most visited places

Catania is the first economic and industrial hub of Sicily. The city is famous for its mainly [petrochemical industry](/wiki/Petrochemical_industry "Petrochemical industry"), and the extraction of [sulphur](/wiki/Sulphur "Sulphur"). In the year 2000, according to Census, Catania was the 14th richest city in Italy, with a GDP of US$6.6 billion (€6.304 billion), which was 0.54% of the Italian GDP, a GDP per capita of US$21,000 (€20,100) and an average GDP per employee of US$69,000 (€66,100).[[79]](#cite_note-80)

In the late-19th century and early-20th century, Catania began to be heavily industrialised, with its several factories and chimneys, often being referred to as Southern Italy's "Manchester".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The economy of Catania suffered heavily from the bad effects of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and was marked by an economic crisis and recession that culminated in the 1920s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Since then, the city lost its industrial and entrepreneurial importance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the 1930s, Catania remained a small fishing town with derelict and disused industries. However, after the destruction of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Catania's economy began to re-grow in the late-1950s and early-1960s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The city's economic growth was so rapid and dynamic that it was often nicknamed the *"Milan of the South"*, or in Italian *"Milano del Sud"*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This rapid economic growth prompted a great number of Sicilians living in the more rural areas, or smaller towns such as Enna, Ragusa and Caltanissetta, to move to the city to seek new jobs.

Today, Catania, despite several problems, has one of the most dynamic economies in the whole of Southern Italy. It still has a strong industrial and agricultural sector, and a fast-growing tourist industry, with many international visitors coming to visit the city's main sights and the nearby Etna volcano. It contains the headquarters or important offices of companies such as [STMicroelectronics](/wiki/STMicroelectronics "STMicroelectronics"), and also several chemical and [pharmaceutical](/wiki/Pharmaceutical "Pharmaceutical") businesses. There have been several new business developments to further boost Catania's economy, including the construction of Etnapolis,[[80]](#cite_note-81) a big [shopping mall](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall") designed by [Massimiliano Fuksas](/wiki/Massimiliano_Fuksas "Massimiliano Fuksas"), the same architect who designed the [FieraMilano](/wiki/FieraMilano "FieraMilano") industrial fair in Milan, or the Etna Valley,[[81]](#cite_note-82) where several high-tech offices are located.

[Tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") is a fast-growing industry in Catania. Lately, the administration and private companies have made several investments in the [hospitality industry](/wiki/Hospitality_industry "Hospitality industry") in order to make tourism a competitive sector in the Metropolitan City. [Etnaland](/w/index.php?title=Etnaland&action=edit&redlink=1 "Etnaland (page does not exist)"), a large [amusement](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park") and [water park](/wiki/Water_park "Water park") located in [Belpasso](/wiki/Belpasso "Belpasso"), is in the metropolitan area of Catania, 12 kilometres (7.5 mi) from the city center. It is the largest of its kind in Southern Italy and attracts thousands of tourists, not only from [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), but also from the rest of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). According to [Tripadvisor](/wiki/Tripadvisor "Tripadvisor") (2018) it is the third-largest water park in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[82]](#cite_note-83)

The seaport of Catania is linked to the road-rail distribution hub of [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"). In September 2020 [Mercitalia Logistics](/wiki/Mercitalia_Logistics "Mercitalia Logistics") opened the first full railway route to link the city to Northern Italy. It replaced an older mixed maritime-railway line.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Catania_BW_2012-10-06_11-26-20.JPG/250px-Catania_BW_2012-10-06_11-26-20.JPG)](/wiki/File:Catania_BW_2012-10-06_11-26-20.JPG)

[Palazzo Centrale dell'Universita](/wiki/Palazzo_dell%27Universit%C3%A0,_Catania "Palazzo dell'Università, Catania"), in city centre, houses the rector's office.

Established in 1434, the [University of Catania](/wiki/University_of_Catania "University of Catania") is the oldest university in Sicily.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Its academic nicknames are: *Siculorum Gymnasium* and *Siciliae Studium Generale*. It hosts 12 faculties and over 62,000 students;[[85]](#cite_note-86) and offers undergraduate and postgraduate programs.

Catania hosts the *[Scuola superiore di Catania](/wiki/Scuola_superiore_di_Catania "Scuola superiore di Catania")*, linked to the University of Catania, aimed at excellence in education; they offer undergraduate and postgraduate programs for teachers.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

Catania is home to the prestigious *Istituto Musicale Vincenzo Bellini*[[87]](#cite_note-88) an advanced institute of musical studies (Conservatory) and the *Accademia di Belle Arti* an advanced institute of artistic studies.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Both institutions offer programs of university level for musical and artistic education.

Opera composer [Vincenzo Bellini](/wiki/Vincenzo_Bellini "Vincenzo Bellini") was born in [Palazzo Gravina-Cruyllas](/wiki/Palazzo_Gravina-Cruyllas "Palazzo Gravina-Cruyllas") in the city center, the palace now houses a museum about him. The [Teatro Massimo Vincenzo Bellini](/wiki/Teatro_Massimo_Bellini "Teatro Massimo Bellini"), which opened in 1890, presents a variety of works through a season, which run from December to May, including the works of its namesake.

[Giovanni Verga](/wiki/Giovanni_Verga "Giovanni Verga") was born in Catania in 1840.[[89]](#cite_note-Verga-90) He became the greatest writer of *[Verismo](/wiki/Verismo_(literature) "Verismo (literature)")*, an Italian [literary movement](/wiki/List_of_literary_movements "List of literary movements") akin to [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(literature) "Naturalism (literature)").[[90]](#cite_note-91) His novels portray life among the lower levels of Sicilian society, such as fishermen and stonemasons, and were written in a mixture of both literary language and the local dialect.[[89]](#cite_note-Verga-90) [Francesco Longo Mancini](/wiki/Francesco_Longo_Mancini "Francesco Longo Mancini") was a painter known for his paintings of nudes, who was born in Catania in 1880.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Candelore-1994.gif/250px-Candelore-1994.gif)](/wiki/File:Candelore-1994.gif)

Saint [Agatha](/wiki/Agatha_of_Sicily "Agatha of Sicily") Festival in February 1994

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Catania_I_cannalori.jpg/250px-Catania_I_cannalori.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catania_I_cannalori.jpg)

[Festival of Saint Agatha](/wiki/Festival_of_Saint_Agatha_(Catania) "Festival of Saint Agatha (Catania)") in 1915

The city's [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") is [Saint Agatha](/wiki/Agatha_of_Sicily "Agatha of Sicily"), who is celebrated with religious pageantry, the [Festival of Saint Agatha](/wiki/Festival_of_Saint_Agatha_(Catania) "Festival of Saint Agatha (Catania)"), on 5 February every year.

The city is the base of the newspaper *[La Sicilia](/wiki/La_Sicilia "La Sicilia")* and of the TV channel [Antenna Sicilia](/wiki/Antenna_Sicilia "Antenna Sicilia"), also known as the [Sicilia Channel](/w/index.php?title=Sicilia_Channel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sicilia Channel (page does not exist)"). Several other [local television](/wiki/Television_channel "Television channel") channels and free-press magazines have their headquarters in Catania.

Catania hosts [Etna Comics](/wiki/Etna_Comics "Etna Comics"), a successful [comic book convention](/wiki/Comic_book_convention "Comic book convention") now in its 12th edition, and the [Catania Tango Festival](/w/index.php?title=Catania_Tango_Festival&action=edit&redlink=1 "Catania Tango Festival (page does not exist)"), an international [Tango](/wiki/Tango "Tango") event. Its 22nd edition has welcomed tango dancers from 27 different countries, confirming the festival as one of the most important international events in its genre.

The city is home to the [Catania Jazz Festival](/wiki/Catania_Jazz_Festival "Catania Jazz Festival"), which typically runs for several winter months with concerts in different locations.[[91]](#cite_note-92) In the late 1980s and during the 1990s Catania had an energetic and unique [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") scene. [Indie pop](/wiki/Indie_pop "Indie pop") and [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") bands, local radio stations, and dynamic [independent music](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") record labels sprung up as a result. As a result, in those years the city experienced a vital and effervescent cultural period. Artists like [Carmen Consoli](/wiki/Carmen_Consoli "Carmen Consoli") and [Mario Venuti](/wiki/Mario_Venuti "Mario Venuti"), and internationally known indie rock bands like [Uzeda](/wiki/Uzeda "Uzeda") came out of this cultural *milieu*.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Stadio_Massimino.jpg/250px-Stadio_Massimino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadio_Massimino.jpg)

Angelo Massimino Football Stadium

Catania is home to many [sports clubs](/wiki/Sports_club "Sports club") covering a wide range of disciplines. The most famous club is the [Catania FC](/wiki/Catania_FC "Catania FC") [football team](/wiki/Football_team "Football team"), followed by approximately half a million supporters.[[92]](#cite_note-93) Another club standing out from the rest is [AS Orizzonte Catania](/wiki/AS_Orizzonte_Catania "AS Orizzonte Catania"), which is the leading women's [water polo](/wiki/Water_polo "Water polo") club in Italy, with 25 National Championship titles (15 in a row from 1992 to 2006), and also in Europe, with 8 European Champions Cup titles.

Catania is the most successful city in team sports in the entire south of Italy (including Sicily and Sardinia), leading (as of August 2025) with 80 National Championships titles, ahead of [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") and of [Bari](/wiki/Bari "Bari").

As for individual sports, 56 athletes from Catania have won world titles, 54 have won European titles and 139 have won national titles.

In the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), over the years, [Italian](/wiki/Italians "Italians") athletes from Catania have won a total of 7 gold medals, 8 silver medals, and 4 bronze medals.

Catania holds the [Catania-Etna](/wiki/Catania-Etna_(Hill_Climb) "Catania-Etna (Hill Climb)") car competition, organized by the [Automobile Club d'Italia](/wiki/Automobile_Club_d%27Italia "Automobile Club d'Italia"). From 1960 to 2011 Catania held the International event named *Trofeo Sant'Agata*, a [road running](/wiki/Road_running "Road running") competition which took place in the streets of the city center, every year on 3 February (the day the [Festival of Saint Agatha](/wiki/Festival_of_Saint_Agatha_(Catania) "Festival of Saint Agatha (Catania)") begins).

## Food and cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Food and cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Arancini_.jpg/250px-Arancini_.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arancini_.jpg)

[Arancini](/wiki/Arancini "Arancini")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Pasta_alla_Norma_%282563876877%29.jpg/250px-Pasta_alla_Norma_%282563876877%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pasta_alla_Norma_(2563876877).jpg)

*[Pasta alla Norma](/wiki/Pasta_alla_Norma "Pasta alla Norma")*

Food is an important part of Catania's culture and way of life. Local cuisine emphasizes several traits of [Sicilian cuisine](/wiki/Sicilian_cuisine "Sicilian cuisine"), whilst developing some of its own character.

[Street food](/wiki/Street_food "Street food") is one of the best ways to experience traditional dishes. [Arancini](/wiki/Arancini "Arancini") are perhaps the city's most iconic: they are stuffed rice balls coated in breadcrumbs and deep fried; in Catania, they are shaped like a cone to remind of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"). Typical specialties from the city include **cipollina** (puff pastry with onion, tomato, and prosciutto filling), *bolognese* (a small pizza topped with tomato, mozzarella, prosciutto, and boiled egg, and covered in puff pastry), **crispelle** (deep fried dough balls with [ricotta](/wiki/Ricotta "Ricotta") or anchovies filling).

During street fairs and religious festivals, street stalls sell **calia e simenza** (toasted chickpeas and pumpkin seeds). Typical from old street markets are **sangeli** (cooked pork blood), **quarumi** (pork tripe), **zuzzu** (pork jelly), **mauru** (edible seaweed), and raw seafood. [Horse meat](/wiki/Horse_meat "Horse meat") is very traditional and is sold in shops called **arrusti e mancia** ("roast it and eat it"), which roast the meat in streetside barbecues.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

Apart from street food, typical dishes from Catania are: *[pasta alla Norma](/wiki/Pasta_alla_Norma "Pasta alla Norma")* (pasta with fried aubergine, tomato sauce and ricotta salata cheese), named after the [namesake opera](/wiki/Norma_(opera) "Norma (opera)") by [Vincenzo Bellini](/wiki/Vincenzo_Bellini "Vincenzo Bellini"); **pasta cco niuru** (pasta in cuttlefish ink), **maccu** (fava beans purée), **bastaddi affucati** or **brocculi affucati** (stewed cauliflower or broccoli), **caponata** (sautéed vegetables) and **scacciata** (a pie filled with tuma cheese) which is traditional during [Christmastime](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas").[[95]](#cite_note-96)

Catania is also famous for its *pasticceria* (pastries and cakes). Pastries vary according to season and to seasonal events: during the [Festival of Saint Agatha](/wiki/Festival_of_Saint_Agatha_(Catania) "Festival of Saint Agatha (Catania)"), patron saint of the city, there are the **cassatelle** (small [cassatas](/wiki/Cassata "Cassata")) and **olivette** (olive shaped [almond paste](/wiki/Almond_paste "Almond paste")). In [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), there are **aceddi ccu l'ovu** (boiled eggs covered in biscuit). In [summer](/wiki/Summer "Summer") there is [granita](/wiki/Granita "Granita"). During the *Festa dei morti* (traditional celebrations in [All Souls' Day](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day")) there are biscuits called **ossa di mortu**, **rame di Napoli** and **nsuddi**.

Drink [kiosks](/wiki/Kiosks "Kiosks") are everywhere in town and serve [soft drinks](/wiki/Soft_drink "Soft drink"). Traditional soft drinks are made by mixing fruit syrups with [soda](/wiki/Carbonated_water "Carbonated water") and other flavors such as [anisette](/wiki/Anisette "Anisette").

Local products include [blood oranges](/wiki/Blood_orange "Blood orange"), [pistachios](/wiki/Pistachio "Pistachio") from [Bronte](/wiki/Bronte,_Sicily "Bronte, Sicily"), extra-virgin [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil"), [cactus fruit](/wiki/Opuntia "Opuntia"), [cherries](/wiki/Cherry "Cherry"), [grapes](/wiki/Grape "Grape") from [Mazzarrone](/wiki/Mazzarrone "Mazzarrone"), [strawberries](/wiki/Strawberry "Strawberry") from [Maletto](/wiki/Maletto "Maletto"), [mushrooms](/wiki/Edible_mushroom "Edible mushroom"), [honey](/wiki/Honey "Honey") and [wine](/wiki/Wine "Wine").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/L%27elettrotreno_M.88-08_alla_stazione_di_Giovanni_XXIII.jpg/250px-L%27elettrotreno_M.88-08_alla_stazione_di_Giovanni_XXIII.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%27elettrotreno_M.88-08_alla_stazione_di_Giovanni_XXIII.jpg)

[Catania Metro](/wiki/Catania_Metro "Catania Metro")

Catania has a commercial seaport ([Catania seaport](/w/index.php?title=Catania_seaport&action=edit&redlink=1 "Catania seaport (page does not exist)")), an [international airport](/wiki/International_airport "International airport") ([Catania Fontanarossa](/wiki/Catania_Fontanarossa "Catania Fontanarossa")), several [railway stations](/wiki/Railway_station "Railway station") ([Catania Centrale](/wiki/Catania_Centrale_railway_station "Catania Centrale railway station") is the main one) and it is the main node of the Sicilian motorway system.

The motorways serving Catania are the A18 [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina")-Catania and the A19 [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo")-Catania; and the prosecution of the A18 going from Catania to [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_Sicily "Syracuse, Sicily") and to [Gela](/wiki/Gela "Gela").

The [Circumetnea](/wiki/Ferrovia_Circumetnea "Ferrovia Circumetnea") is a [narrow-gauge](/wiki/Narrow-gauge "Narrow-gauge") railway that runs for 110 km (68 mi) from Catania around the base of [Mount Etna](/wiki/Mount_Etna "Mount Etna"). It attains an elevation of 976 m (3,202 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level") before descending to rejoin the coast at [Giarre](/wiki/Giarre "Giarre")-[Riposto](/wiki/Riposto "Riposto") to the north.

In the late 1990s, the first line of an [underground railway](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") (*[Metropolitana di Catania](/wiki/Metropolitana_di_Catania "Metropolitana di Catania")*) was built. The underground service started in 1999 and it is currently active on a route of 8.8 km (5.5 mi), from the station Nesima (west of town), passing through the stations of San Nullo, Cibali, Milo, Borgo, Giuffrida, Italia, Galatea, Giovanni XXIII, to Stesicoro. The last two stations, bringing Catania's underground into the city centre, opened on 20 December 2016.[[97]](#cite_note-Subway-98) The line is planned to be extended from the [satellite city](/wiki/Satellite_city "Satellite city") of [Paternò](/wiki/Patern%C3%B2 "Paternò") to [Fontanarossa Airport](/wiki/Catania_Fontanarossa "Catania Fontanarossa").

### Catania public transport statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Catania public transport statistics")]

The average amount of time people spend commuting on public transit in Catania on a weekday is 56 min. 13% of public transit riders ride for more than 2 hours every day. The average amount of time people wait at a stop or station for public transit is 23 min, while 46% of riders wait for over 20 minutes on average every day. The average distance people usually ride in a single trip with public transit is 4.7 km (2.9 mi), while 3% travel for over 12 km (7.5 mi) in a single direction.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

### Influence on the planning of Adelaide, Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catania&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Influence on the planning of Adelaide, Australia")]

The site of what was to become the major [Australian](/wiki/Australia "Australia") city of [Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide "Adelaide") was surveyed and laid out by Colonel William Light, the first Surveyor-General of South Australia. In 1823, Light had fondly written of Catania: "The two principal streets cross each other at right angles in the square in the direction of north and south and east and west. They are wide and spacious and about a mile [1.6 km] long". This became the basis for his plan of Adelaide.[[102]](#cite_note-103)
